text: AHGUAY, a town on the Slave coast of W. Africa, 8 m. W of Popoe. Cotton and indigo are grown in the neighbourhood; and salt is manufactured. An extensive lagune runs behind the town, at half-amile's distance from the sea.—Duncan.
AH-HEL-DESSEH, a river of Arctic America, which flows from the SW extremity of Artillery lake into the NE extremity of Great Slave lake, at Fort Reliance, in N lat. 62° 46′, W long. 109° 1′.

AHINAGH, a p. of Ireland, in co. Cork. Area 9,420 acres. Pop. 2,699. AHIOULI, AKHYOLI, or ANKHIA'LU, a port of Romelia, in the pashalik of Silistria, at the entrance of the gulf of Burgház, 9 m. ENE of Burghaz. It stands on a projecting point of the same name, 6 m. SSW 1 W from Mesembria, in N lat. 42° 32', E long. 27° 40'. It is the Anchialus of Arrian.
AHKAF, a name given in some maps to the southern part of the Great Arabian desert.
AHLAY-GHEWN, or ALA-KYUN, an island formed above the junction of the Kyan-Duayn with the Irawaddi, in Birmah.
AHLBECK, a fishing v. in the isle of Usedom, in the circle of Stettin in Pomerania.
AHLBERG, a v. in the electorate of Hesse-Cassel, circle of Hof-Geismar, 9 m. N of Cassel, celebrated for its extensive magnesian works.

AHLDEN, a bailiwick of Hanover, in the principality of Luneburg, lying along the Aller at its junction with the Leine and Bohme. It has an area of 18 sq. m. and consists chiefly of low and sandy ground. -The town of A. is situated on the Old Leine, 6 m. S of Walsrode. Pop. 750. The unfortunate Sophia Dorothea, daughter of the duke of Zell, and consort of George I., was immured in the palace or castle of A. from 1694 till her death, on 2d Nov., 1726, on a charge—now known to be false,—of an intrigue with Count Königsmark.
AHLDORF, a town of Würtemberg, on the Neckar, in the Schwartzwald. Pop. 864.

AHLEN, a town of Prussia, in the circle of Munster, on the r. bank of the Werse, 18 m. SE of Munster. Pop. 2,550, chiefly linen-weavers. It is near the junction of the Hanover, Elberfeld, and Dusseldorf railway with the branch to Munster.
AHLERSTEDT, a town of Hanover, in the prov. of Bremen, 13 m. S of Stade.
AHLFELD, or ALFELD, a town and bailiwick of Hanover, in the landrost of Hildesheim, on the Leine, between Eimbeck and Elze. Pop. 2,650.
AHLSTADT, a v. of Saxe-Coburg, N of Rodach. Pop. 306.

AHMAR (EL), a halting-place on the southern caravan-route from Genneh to Kosseir, in Upper Egypt, in N lat. 25° 56', E long. 33° 40'. Also a halting-place in the Sahara between Mafras and Omah. It is so called from the red colour of the neighbouring hills.—Denham.
AHMAR (KOUM EL), a town of Upper Egypt, on the I. bank of the Nile, opposite to El-Kab.
AHMAR, or HAMRAH (RAS-EL), or CAPO DI

GUARDIA, a point of land forming the NW extremity of the bay of Bona, on the coast of Algiers; in N lat. 36° 58', E long. 7° 47'. It receives its Arabian name from the red or pinkish colour of the marble of which it is formed.
AHMED, or Hami'd, a Turkoman v. of 50 houses, in Kurdistán, in about N lat. 39° 30′, E long. 34° 5′. Alt. above sea-level 2,700 ft.—Ainsworth.
AHMED, or Hamid (Beni), a town of Central Egypt, near the l. bank of the Nile, 6 m. S by E of Mineh.
AHMED (KASR), a v. of Upper Egypt, near the r. bank of the Nile, opposite Asfun.

AHMED-ABAD, a city and district of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gujarat, on the E side of the Saubermutti river, and to the NE of the peninsula of Gujarat. The district receives its name—signifying 'the Abode of Ahmed'-from the fortified city of Ahmed-abad, once the Mahommedan capital of the kingdom of Gujarat, which is situated on the r. bank of the Saubermutti, in N lat. 23° 1', E long. 72° 42'. This city was founded in 1426 by Ahmed Shah. Tieffenthaler says it was founded on the site of Assaul. Colonel Tod is of opinion that it was built from the materials of Chandravati and Anhulwassa. In its greatest splendour, it covered, with its suburbs, an area of 27 m. in circumference; even in the 17th cent., when Thevenot visited it, it was reckoned 20 m. in circuit. With the decline of the Mogul power, the nawabs of A. assumed independent power; but they soon fell under the sway of the Mahrattas, whose extortions and heavy fiscal regulations reduced it to the mere shadow of what it once was as a commercial city. In 1779, the British troops took possession I. AHO of A.; but it was restored to the Peishwa in 1783, and retained by him till the final dissolution of his power in 1818, when it was taken into the permanent possession of the E. I. company. A. suffered considerably from an earthquake in 1819, and it had been terribly devastated by pestilence in 1812; but its pop. was roughly estimated in 1820 at 100,000. It is surrounded by a high wall with irregular towers every 50 yards, and has 12 principal gates. Its distance, by dak, from Bombay is 321 m.; from Poona 389; from Delhi 610; from Calcutta by Oojein 1,234.
AHMED-ABAD, a v. in Khorásan, 14 m. NNE of Sabzawár.
AHMED BEGLU', a considerable v. in the Persian prov. of Azerdbijan, between Ahar and Ardabil.
AHMED EYEA, a v. on the Midan-Dagh, in the district of Ghilan in Azerdbijan.
AHMEDIYAH, a large village in the pashalik of Baghdad, on the river of Dura, SE of Mardin.
AHMED KHAN, a town of Afghanistan, on the route from Quetta to Ghuzni.
AHMED-KHAN'S-TANDA, a large v. of Sinde, 68 m. NE of Kurrachi, in N lat. 25° 27'.

AHMEDNUGGUR, a city and district in the prov. of Aurungabad, in the presidency of Bombay. The district has an area of 9,900 sq. m., forming one of the most elevated and temperate regions of the Deccan. The land-revenue in 1827-8 was £181,584. —The city, situated in an extensive plain watered by the Soona, was founded in 1493 by Ahmed Nizam Shah, who made it his capital. After the death of Aurungzebe, it was seized by the Mahrattas. Dowlut Row Scindia captured it in 1797, but was forced to cede it in 1803 to the E. I. company. It is now the head-station of a civil, military, and judicial establishment; and its fortress, the palace of its former sultans, is considered one of the strongest in India. The pop. in 1820 was estimated at 20,000, exclusive of the garrison.—A. is 27 m. SW of Aurungabad; 83 m. dâk distance, NE from Poona; 181 m. by Poona from Bombay; and 1,119 from Calcutta.
AHMEDPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, 11 m. S of Juggernaut.—Also a v. in the Punjab, in N lat. 30° 34'.

AHMEDPUR-BARRA, a city in Bhawlpur, 30 m. SW of the city of Bhawlpur. It lies on very low ground, on the W bank of a large canal called Kœtubawah, which irrigates the country all round it. Hough estimates the pop. at 30,000; Atkinson at only 10,000; Lieutenant Trevelyan supposes A. may contain 6,000 houses. It has a good bazar, and a large mosque. On the W side is a mud fort. "The exceeding lowness of the houses-which look still lower from being flat-roofed—and the small shops give the city rather a mean appearance" [Trevelyan]; yet A. is said to possess manufactures of matchlocks, powder, cotton, and silk. - Wood. - Masson. - Lieut. Trevelyan in Calcutta Journal, 1835. AHMEDPUR CHUTA—i. e. 'A. the Less,' so called to distinguish it from the above city—a town of Bhawlpur, 65 m. SW of Ahmedpur-Barra; in N lat. 28° 17′, E long. 69° 48′.
AHMEIDAT, a v. in the pashalik of Mosul, 8 m. NW of Mosul, on a high precipitous bank overhanging the Tigris, which here forms a chain of islands 3 m. in length.—Forbes.
AHMU'D, or Amo'd, a pergunnah of Gujarat, 20 m. N by W from Broach. The soil, which is a rich black loam, produces the best cotton in the province.
AHN, a town of Luxemburg, on the Mosel, S of Grevenmachern.
AHOGHILL, a p. of Ireland, in co. Antrim, on the W side of the Bann. Area 35,419 acres. Pop. 23,622. The v. of A. is on the l. bank of a small tributary of the Maine, 10 m. NNW of Antrim.

AHOME, an Indian race in Mexico, in the prov. of Cinaloa. They are said to be of less barbarous manners than the other Indian tribes of New Spain. Their principal settlement is at the mouth of the Fuerte, on the gulf of California.—Alcedo.
AHORCADOS (Lor), a group of islets in the Pacific, off the coast of Ecuador, in S lat 1° 40', W long. 80° 40'.
AHORNBERG, a v. of Bavaria, in the bailiwick of Munchberg, circle of the Upper Maine.
AHOU, a v. of Persia, in Kirman, on the route from Kirman to Bunderabbas, on the S side of the Jebel Abad.

AHR, or Aar, a river of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine. It rises in the Eifelberg, near Alendorf; runs SSW, E, and NE; and falls into the Rhine, on the l. bank between Remagen and Sinzig, opposite Linz, after a course of 30 m. through the beautiful and fertile Ahrgau. The A. is a scanty stream in summer; but in winter is often a swollen and furious torrent. It is celebrated for its trout and craw-fish. The wines of the Ahrgau resemble Moselle in quality.
AHRAM, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Fars, 27 m. SE of Bushire, on the road to Firozeabad.
AHRENSBERG, or Arensberg, a v. of Prussia, in the circle of Königsberg, NE of Kreuzburg.
AHRENSBOECK, a town of Denmark, in Holstein, between Lubeck and Ploen. Pop. 700.
AHRENSBURG, a town of Denmark, in Holstein, NE of Altona.—Also a v. in the principality of Schauenburg-Lippe, 6 m. ESE of Bückeburg
AHRENSDORF, a v. of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, 6 m. SE of Potsdam. Pop. 225.—Also a v. of Hanover, in the prov. of Lunenburg, 4 m. W of Ebsdorf.

AHRN, a stream in the Tyrol, which, rising at the S foot of the Drei Herrn Spitz, flows SW, and S by E, through the romantic Ahrnthal to Taufers, and then S through the wider Tauferthal, to Brunecken, where it joins the Rienz on the r. bank.

AHRWEILER, a town of Prussia, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, prettily situated on the Ahr, 12 m. from Remagen. The church is a fine old Gothic edifice. Pop. 2,500, chiefly employed in the cultivatiou of the surrounding vineyards, and in the manufacture and export of the wine of the Ahrgau. The Ahrbleichet or pale red wine of Ahr, made in this neighbourhood, is highly esteemed.

AHSA, or Hassa (EL), a small stream in the district of Djebal, which flows through the deep wady or ravine of the same name into the Dead sea, 38 m. NE of Shobek. Messrs. Irby and Mangles are inclined to identify it with the Zared of Scripture.
AHSA, or Hassa (EL), a small town of Arabia, on the Afta river, 75 m. SW of Lachsa.
AHTAREEN, or 'Ak-Devarin, a v. in the pashalik and 17 m. NE of the city of Aleppo, in the district of Hailán.
AHTEBOLI. See AKTEBOLI.
AHUARICA, a river of Ecuador, rising on the eastern declivity of the Cordilleras, and flowing SE into the Napo, above San Miguel, in S lat. 1° 48'.
AHUILLE, a commune and v. of France, in the dep. of Mayenne. Pop. 1,408.
AHULI, a v. of Abyssinia, 35 m. S of Lake Aussa, where there are several hot springs.—Isenberg.
AHUN, a cant., com. and v. of France in the dep. of Creuse. The com. and v. lie on a hill near the Creuse, 13 m. SE of Gueret. Pop. 2,212.
AHUUS, or AHUYS, a v. of Sweden, at the mouth of the Helge An, 9 m. SSE of Christianstadt, of which it is the port.

AHWA'Z, or AHWUZ, or HAWA'Z, a once celebrated but now decayed city of Persia, in the prov. | AIA of Khuzistán, on the E bank of the Karun, 48 m. SW of Shushter, 20 m. W of Waïs, and 92 m. NE of Bussorah; in N lat. 31° 18½, and about E long. 49°. [Selby.] The modern town, built on the site of the ancient Aginis, occupies but a small portion of the ruinclad area of the old city, and can only boast of a single mosque. "A collection of hovels rather than houses, built of the stones which once formed a part of the city on whose site it now stands,—a barren desert on every side,—vestiges of canals which once irrigated and carried plenty through the whole of this then productive country, -water-mills formerly used to grind the corn and press the sugar-canes which the country abundantly produced, but now neglected and useless,—are all that remain of this once great and important city." [Lieut. Selby.] A. is inhabited by Rayats, subject to the Chab sheikh. The pop. does not exceed 1,600. Several of the inhabitants trade between Mohammerah and Shushter. They usually take 3 days in descending, without the aid of sails, from Hasamabad, a v. between 4 and 5 m. from Shushter, to A.; and between 4 and 6 days from A. to Mohammerah. At an early period, a bund of great strength had been thrown across the river here, probably for the purposes of irrigation; and its remains are still so considerable as to render it frequently necessary for boats to discharge their cargoes before attempting to pass it. This bund or dam stopped the Euphrates steamer in her projected ascent of the river in 1836; but Lieut. Selby found an opening of about 40 yards wide at the western end of it, through which nearly the whole of the river—then about 200 yards broad, just above it—rushes with great velocity and a considerable fall, and effected a passage, in the steamer Assyria, through this channel by means of a hawser and tackle. Mr. Layard says that the river at A. is between a quarter and half-a-mile in breadth, and has a continuous channel of above 8 ft. deep even in the driest season of the year. He is of opinion that the passage might in general be effected without any great difficulty by a small steamer.—A. is thought to have attained its highest prosperity under the earlier khaliffs of the house of Abbas; its decadence was far advanced in the middle of the 12th century. The 2d vol. of the Asiatic Society's Transactions contains an interesting paper on the ruins of Ahwaz, with notes by Captain Taylor; and in the 14th vol. of the Geographical Journal, Lieut. Selby has given a full account of his ascent of the Karún to Shushter, in 1842.

AHWENDEAR, an islet in the Red sea; in N lat. 26° 36′, E long. 36° 10′; 6½ m. WSW of Sherm Demerah.—Moresby. AI, a river of Russia, an important affluent of the Ufa, which rises, in several head-streams, to the W of Mount Jurma, in the Ural chain, in the prov. of Orenburg, and after a course first SW and then NW, falls into the Ufa, on the l. bank, 15 m. below Meteli. The upper part of its valley is narrow and marshy; below the bend where it turns to the W it is deep, narrow, and rocky.—Murchison. AI, or Ay, a v. of Bavaria, in the circle of the Upper Danube, N of Illertissen. Pop. 167.

AIA (CAPE), or AYA-BARUN, a bluff bold headland on the SW coast of the Crimea, in N lat. 44° 25', E long. 33° 29', 4 m. to the WNW of Cape Saritche, consisting of a marmoraceous limestone rock, presenting rough clefts pervaded by a reddish clay. It has an alt. of about 1,200 ft.—Pallas.

AIAGAS, or Ayaguz, a river of Mongolia, which takes its rise on the NW flanks of the Aiagou mountains, a branch of the Ulugh-Dagh, and pursues a N, NW, and SW course to the Balkhash lake or Balkasi-Nur. It is included, in Arrowsmith's map, within the boundaries of the Russian government of Omsk.

AIAGHA-DAGH, or ZAGROS, an extensive range MARKERANANANANANANANANANANANANANANANANANANAN of mountains which may be described generally as forming the eastern boundary of the basin of the Tigris. Fraser designates this chain "the backbone of Kurdistan," and regards it as stretching from "the knot of mountains in Armenia where the Tigris and Euphrates take their rise, to the Bakhtiari mountains that run past Ispahan, and, passing through Fars, unite with those of Kermán and Mekrán." The name, however, is generally given with special reference to that portion of the Kurdistan mountains which lies to the south of the 36th parallel, forming the boundary betwixt Turkish and Persian Kurdistan, and the watershed betwixt the rivers flowing SW to the Tigris, and SE to the Karasu. The principal passes in the range thus defined, beginning from the N, are the Kurtek pass to the S of the Little Zab river, leading southwards from the territory of Uardasht to the town of Sulimania; the Bannah pass; the Naukhan pass; the Garrun pass, leading eastwards from Sulimania to Senna in Ardelan; and the Karina pass between Holwan and Kermanshah. To the S of the latter pass, little is accurately known of the country stretching southwards to the western declivity of the Hamrin hills.
AIALA. See SAKARIA. AIANDUN, ANDOUNE, or AYANDUN, a v. of Natolia, on the coast of the Black sea, 20 m. E of Ineboli. There is a copper-mine in the neighbourhood of this village.
AIAR-KHAZBA, a v. of Great Bukharia, 120 m. ESE of Samarcand.

AIAS, a town of about 600 houses, in Anatolia, on the route from Constantinople to Angora. It stands on a small stream called the Aias-su, an affluent of the Kirmir, and is celebrated for its warm springs.— Also a seaport in the district of Karamania, on a long narrow bay of the same name on the W side of the gulf of Iskenderun. It probably marks the site of the ancient Ægæ, of which the present name—which Beaufort prefers to write, as pronounced, Ayas—appears to be a corruption. Lat. 36° 46' N.—Beaufort. AIASALUK, AYA'SOLU'GH, OF AIAZLIK, a v. of Asia Minor, 13 hours from Smyrna, and half-a-mile to the NE of Ephesus, out of the ruins of which it appears to have been originally constructed, though now consisting only of a few miserable huts halfburied in thickets of tamarinds and fragments of ancient masonry. The name, according to Van Egmont, signifies 'the Temple of the Moon.' Some consider it a corruption of Agios Theologos, the name given by the Greek church to the beloved disciple, whose tomb is supposed to have been here, and to whose memory a magnificent church, now a mouldering mosque, was here dedicated. The modern name seems to point rather to this latter edifice—the Agios Basilicos—than to the ecclesiastical title of the venerable apostle.—Pococke.—Arundell.

AIASMATI, a v. of Anatolia, 12 m. W of Pergamos, near a wide but shallow stream called the Aiasmati-shai. It is a small place of only 140 houses. Choiseul supposes it may stand on the site of Attea; but Arundell saw nothing about it to support this conjecture.

ALASMUN, or AYAZMEND, a town of Natolia, in the sanjak of Karahsi, one day's journey S of Adramyt, and within 6 m. of the coast of the gulf opposite Mitylene.—There is a small port of the same name, in the sanjak of Khudávendikar, on the S coast of the sea of Marmora.
AIA TEKLA, a v. of Cyprus, on the road from Limasol to Nicosia, 14 m. S of the latter town.
AIBAKHA, a river of Mongolia, which rises in the Sounite territory on the SE skirts of the Gobi, and flows southwards, probably to join the Koundu- 'un, a branch of the Hoangho. AIG
AIBAR, a v. in the Crimea, 26 versts [17 m.] S of Dürmeen, on the direct route from Perekop to Acmetchet.—Pallas.
AIBLING, a town of Bavaria, in the Up. Bav. circle, near the confluence of the Mangfall and the Glon, on the road from Munich to Salsburg. Pop. 1,280. It is the ancient Albianum.
AICH, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Schwarzwalder circle, on a small stream of the same name, which, rising near Holzgerlingen, flows into the Neckar near Ober-Ensingen. Pop. of town 829.

AICHA, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 20 m. NNE of Jung Bunzlau, on the r. bank of the Jeschkenbaches. Pop. 1,306, chiefly linen-weavers. Between this town and Oschitz runs a curious basaltic ridge known as the Devil's wall.

AICHACH, a fortified town of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Bavaria, 3 m. NE of Friedberg, on the Paar. Pop. 1,570. A. was taken and burned by the Swedes in the Thirty-years' war; and in 1704 by the English. Lat. 48° 28' N.
AICHBERG. See EGGENBERG.
AICHSTADT. See EICHSTADT.
AIDAK, or OGURTCHINSK, a small island in the Caspian sea, off the coast of Kharasm, in N lat. 39°, E long. 53° 20'.

AIDENBACH, or AITENBACH, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Bavaria, 6 m. SW of Vilshofen on the Aderbach. Pop. 615. The Bavarians were defeated by the Austrians near this place in 1706. Lat. 48° 35′ N.
AIDHAB, or MERSA HALAIB, a port of the Red sea, on the Nubian coast, in N lat. 22° 12′, E long. 36° 48′.

AIDI'N, a sanjak of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Anatolia; bounded by the sanjak of Sarukhan on the N; Kutahiyah on the E; Mentesha on the S; and Saghlah on the W. Its capital is Tireh; and its other principal towns are Bainder, Aidin, Azineh, Alashehr or Philadelphia, Yenishehr Aidin, and Sart or Sardes.—The town of Aidin, or Guzel-hissar, [i. e. 'the Beautiful castle,'] is finely situated on the S declivity of Mount Messogis, 12 hours E by S from Ephesus. The Mæander flows to the S of it, leaving a fertile plain of light sandy soil betwixt it and the town which is covered with fig-orchards.

AIDINJIK, a town of Asiatic Turkey, near the coast of the sea of Marmora, 32 m. WNW of Ulubad. The town is full of ancient fragments brought from the ruins of Cyzicus, and the environs are highly cultivated.—Hamilton.

AIDONATI, or AIDANAT, a ruined Greek monastery, on the banks of the Acheron, a little to the SW of Suli. The remains of an ancient temple are scattered round this place; and the name appears to preserve the remembrance of ancient tradition; for Aidoneus, or Pluto, is fabled to have carried off the beautiful daughter of Ceres from Sicily to this very region.—Hughes.

AIDO'S, a town of Bulgaria, in the sanjak of Silistria, 20 m. ENE of Carnabat, at the mouth of a defile which was adopted by the Russians in crossing the Balkan during their last campaign against Turkey.
AIGALIES, or AIGALIERS, a v. of France in the dep. of Gard, 15 m. N of Nîmes. Pop. 302.
AIGELDINGEN, or EIGELTINGEN, a town of Baden, between Aach and Stockach. Pop. 853.

AIGEN, a v. of Upper Austria, in the Mühlcircle 30 m. NNW of Linz.—Also a castle and park of the prince of Schwarzenberg, 43 m. from Salzburg, at the foot of the Gaisberges, the highest peak of which has an alt. of 4,000 ft., and commands a noble view, embracing the Chiemsee, and seven other mountain-lakes.
AIGINA. See ÆGINA.
AI GIORGHIO, or AGHIO GIORGHIO, a barren rocky islet of Asia Minor, the largest of a group of such islets, between Kalamaki bay and Kastelorizo; m N lat. 36° 8½', E long. 29° 30½'.

AIGLE, or AELEN, a prefectship of Switzerland, in the canton of Vaud, in the valley and on the E side of the Rhone. Pop. 12,824. This district, which comprises nine large parishes, was conquered from Savoy by Bern in 1475, at which time it was comprised within the Pays de Vaud. In 1803, when the Pays de Vaud was constituted a sovereign canton, A. went with it. The natives speak French. Its capital, of the same name, is situated on the road from Martigny to Lausanne, 9 m. N of Saint-Maurice, on a river called the Grande Eau. Pop. 2,200. The houses are built of rough black marble, of which there are quarries in the vicinity. There are brine-pits in the neighbourhood which yield a whiter and heavier salt than those of Bevieux. It is the Aquileia of the Middle ages.

AIGLE (L'), a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Orne, and arrond. of Mortagne, on the Rille river, 37 m. NE of Alençon. The town is pleasantly situated on the slope of two hills, and is surrounded by a wall and ditch. It has several thriving manufactures, particularly that of pins, and of sewing and knitting needles. Pop. 4,712.—There is a cape of the same name, on the coast of the Mediterranean, between Marseilles and Toulon; in N lat. 43° 10′, E long. 5° 36′.
AIGLE (Joug de L'), a mountain of Sardinia in the chain of the Cottian Alps, having an alt. of 1,209 toises, or 7,731 ft. above sea-level.—H. de Thury.
AIGLEPIERRE, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Jura, 10 m. NE of Poligny. Pop. 514.
AIGNAN, a cant. of France, in the dep. of Gers, and arrond. of Mirande. Its chief town, of the same name, is 22 m. NW of Mirande. Pop. 1,771.

AIGNAN (SAINT), a cant. of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, and arrond. of Blois. Its chief town of the same name, on the l. bank of the Cher, 24 m. S of Blois, has a pop. of 2,772, and is a place of considerable trade. In the environs there is an important quarry of gun-flints, which are also quarried in the communes of Meusnes, Couffy, and Chatillon.— There are a com. and v. of the same name in the dep. of Loire-Inférieure, arrond. of Nantes. Pop. 1,288. -Also in the dep. of Morbihan, arrond. of Pontivy. Pop. 1,209.—Also in the dep. of Sarthe, arrond. of Mamers. Pop. 973.—The name was also given by Bougainville to an island 30 m. long, in the archipelago of Louisiade; in Slat. 10° 41', E long. 152° 5'.
AIGNAN-EN-LASSAY (SAINT), a com. and v. of France, in the dep. and arrond. of Mayenne. Pop. 1,201.
AIGNAN-SUR-ROE' (SAINT), a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, arrond. of Chateau-Gontier. The village, which is 18 m. W of Chateau-Gontier, had a pop. of 574 in 1841.
AIGNANT (SAINT), a com. of France, in the dep. of La Creuse, arrond. of Aubrisson. Pop. 1,244.
AIGNANT-DE-VERSILLAC, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Creuse, arrond. of Gueret. Pop. 2,162.
AIGNAY, a town of France, in the dep. of La Côte d'Or, in the arrond. and 25 m. SSE of Chatillonsur-Seine. Pop. 947.
AIGNE', a com. of France, in the dep. of La Sarthe, arrond. of Mans. Pop. 819.

AIGRE, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Charente, and arrond. of Ruffec. The com. and v. are 12 m. SW of Ruffec. Pop. 1,564. Brandy is extensively manufactured here; and 12 well-frequented fairs are held in the course of the year.
AIGREFEUILLE, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep, of Charente-Inférieure, arrond. of Roche- AIG fort. The com. and v., 11 m. SE of Nantes, had a pop. of 1,369 in 1840.

AIGUEBELLE, a town of Savoy, on the l. bank of the Arc, near its junction with the Isère, 16 m. NNW of St Jean de Maurienne, on the road from Chambéry up the valley of the Arc and across Mount Cenis, into Sardinia. Alt. above sea-level 1,055 ft. [Saussure.] Pop. 1,300. Its position on the high road to Italy has rendered it a place of some historical importance. It first appears in history under the name of Carbonaria, which it exchanged in the 5th cent. for that of Aquabella. Destroyed by the Saracens in 835, it was rebuilt in 998. The princes of Savoy made it their residence for several successive centuries. In 1742, the confederate forces of France and Spain defeated the Sardinians in the neighbourhood of A.
AIGUEBELLETTE, a v. of Savoy, beautifully situated on one of the routes from Pont Beauvoisin to Chambéry, on a small mountain-lake of the same name.
AIGUEBLANCHE, a v. of Savoy, in the Tarentaise, on the r. bank of the Isère. Pop. 415.
AIGUEFONDE, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Tarn, and arrond. of Castres. Pop. 2,044.

AIGUEPERSE, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Puy-de-Dôme, arrond. of Riom. Pop. of the cant. in 1831, 15,037. The town is a well-built place, situated on the river Beron, 8 m. NNE of Riom. Pop. 3,053. It was the birth-place of the chancellor D'Aguesseau, and of the abbè Delille. Its name is a corruption of Aquasparsa, its earlier designation from the numerous streams in the vicinity. -There is a com. and v. of the same name in the arrond. of Villefranche in the dep. of the Rhone, 6 m. NW of Monsols.
AIGUES, a river of France, which rises on the SW frontier of the dep. of Hautes Alpes, crosses the dep. of Drôme in a westerly course, passes Nions, and flows into the Rhone 5 m. W of Orange.
AIGUES-BONNES, a v. of France, in the dep. of the Aude, 2 m. SW of Caudies, so called from a mineral spring in the vicinity.
AIGUESCAUDES. See EAUX CHAUDES.

AIGUES-MORTES, a town of France, in the dep. of Gard, and arrond. of Nîmes, 4 m. from the mouth of the Grau-du-Roi, and at the junction of the canal of Beaucaire with that of Etangs. It is 5 m. S of Lunel, and 20 m. SSW of Nîmes; in N lat. 43° 34', E long. 4° 11'. [Conn. des Temps.] Pop. of cant. and t. 3,393. It is surrounded by salt-marshes and lagunes, formed apparently by the retirement of the sea in this quarter; and numerous shoal banks in the bight of A. render it an unsafe anchorage. Its houses are all of one story only, so as to be under the shelter of the ramparts; its ancient walls and gates are yet entire, but the fosse has been filled up. Salt is largely manufactured here from the salt-marshes of Peccais to the SE of the town, and forms the chief article of commerce. In 1837, the shipping which entered and sailed from the port of A. amounted to 24,453 tons; the customs receipts to 67,761 francs.—St. Louis embarked at the harbour of this place, on his expedition to the Holy Land, in 1269.
AIGUES-VIVES, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Gard, and cant. of Sommières, 12 m. SW of Nimes. Pop. 1,618.

AIGUILLE, a name frequently borne by those sharp-pointed mountains of the Alps whose summits, shooting up in an acute form, and often to a great height, resemble immense obelisks or needles. They are composed of "nearly vertical plates or beds of granite" [Bakewell], and are distinguished from those great masses of rock, like rude pyramids, which are commonly known as Pics, by their comparatively slender and pillar-like outline. Bakewell's theory of their formation is, that after having been upheaved by some vast force to their present elevation, with their edges and summits broken into irregular forms by the same force, "the moisture of the atmosphere, which subsequently penetrated between the perpendicular fissures, would split and disintegrate the masses on their sides, without diminishing much of their height." [Travels in the Alps, vol. I., p. 375.] The following sketch of the A. du Drua, and A. Vert b. as seen in approaching the source of the Arveiron, is from Mr. Bakewell's work. The following is a list of the principal aiguilles:- A. VE T, connected with the A. du Dru by a narrow feet. ridge of rocks,
AIGUILLES, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Hautes Alpes, on the Guil river, 18 m. SE of Brian on, celebrated for its cheeses.

AIGUILLON, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Lot-et-Garonne, near the confluence of these rivers, 14 m. NW of Agen. Pop. of com. 4,079, of whom about 2,000 are in the town. Its ancient name was Aculeionum or Aiguillionum. An English army successfully held the castle of A. against John, Duke of Normandy, with an army of 60,000, in 1346.—There is a small port of the same name in Vendée, in the arrond. of Fontenayle-Comte, on the l. bank of the Lay, at its influx into the Pertuis-Breton. Pop. 848.—Also a cape in the dep. of Var, on the W side of the gulf of Napoule.
AIGUILLON (Pic D'), a mountain of Spain, on the northern face of the Pyrennean chain, in N lat. 42° 47', W long. 0° 8'. Alt. 9,685 ft.—Reboul and Vidal.
AIGUINES, a town of France, in the dep. of Var, 16 m. NE of Barjols, near the Verdon. Pop. 900.
AIGULIN (SAINT), a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, and arrond. of Jonzac. Pop. 1,406.
AIGURANDE, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Indre, arrond. of Châtre. Pop. of cant. in 1831, 11,759; of com., 1,859. It is the centre of an extensive cattle-trade.
AIKTON, a p. in Cumberland, 8 m. SW of Carlisle. Area 5,270 acres. Pop. 802.
AIKURVE', a remarkable isolated rock rising to a great height on the S of the Tuarutu mountains, near the sources of the Takutu river, in British Guiana.

AILAH, or AILATH, a ruined town of Arabia, of which the remains are reported to exist at the northern extremity of the Elanitic gulf, 2 m. N of the present v. of Akaba. It is the Aila of Ibn Haukal, who describes it as having been "formerly a small town with some fruitful lands about it." Makrizi describing it, says: "it is from hence that the Hedjaz begins. In former times it was the frontier-place of AIM the Greeks." "Near Aila," he adds, "was formerly situated a large and handsome town called Asziorus." There seems little reason to doubt that these two places are the Elath and Eziongeber of 1 Kings ix. 26.
AILA'T, the chief v. in the valley of Modat in Abyssinia, 20 m. from Massouah.
AILLANT-SUR-THOLON, a town of France, in the dep. of the Yonne, arrond. of Joigny, 10 m. NW of Auxerre. Pop. 800.
AILLAS, a com. of France, in the dep. of Gironde, arrond. of Bazas, 6 m. NE of Bazas. Pop. 2,061.
AILLE, a river of France, an affluent of the Argens, in the dep. of Var. It has a course of about 18 m., of which 7 are navigable in winter.
AILLEVILLERS, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, and arrond. of Lure, 6 m. NW of Luxeuil. Pop. 2,767. There are some iron furnaces here.
AILLIANVILLE, a com. and v. of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, arrond. of Chaumont. Pop. 486.
AILLON, a com. and v. of Savoy, 10 m. NE of Chambery. Pop. 1,840. Iron is wrought here.
AILLY-LE-HAUT-CLOCHER, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of the Somme, and arrond. of Abbeville. The v. is 7 m. ESE of Abbeville. Pop. 800. Pop. of the cant., 12,645.

AILLY-SUR-NOYE, a cant., com., and v. of France, in the dep. of Somme, and arrond. of Montdidier. The v. is 13 m. NW of Montdidier, on the Noye, an affluent of the Avre. It is a station on the railroad from Paris to Abbeville, 6 m. from Amiens, and 22 from Abbeville.
AILRINGEN, a v. in Wurtemberg, in the Jaxt circle, and on the Jaxt river. Pop. 373.

AILSA, a huge insulated rock in the mouth of the frith of Clyde, between the coasts of Ayrshire and Kintyre in Scotland; in N lat. 55° 15', W long. 5° 7'. [Galbraith.] It is a mass of columnar syenitic trap rising in some parts nearly 400 ft. perpendicularly, and then ascending in the form of a cone 700 ft.; having a total alt. of 1,100 ft. from sea-level to its apex. Its base is of an elliptical form; 3,300 ft. in the major axis, by 2,200 ft. in the minor. Its columnar cliffs on the W and SW sides, are literally covered during the summer-months by myriads of solan geese, cormorants, puffins, links, gulls, and other sea-birds. Goats and rabbits are also reared upon it. It is accessible only on the NE side.
AILU, one of the Radack group of islands in the N. Pacific, in N lat. 10° 8′, E long. 170°. Kotzebue describes it as scarcely a mile in length, or a quarter of a mile in breadth.

AILY, or AILLY, a headland on the northern coast of France, 5 m. W by N 1/2 N of Dieppe. It is surmounted by a light 305 ft. above the sea, revolving every 80 seconds. Lat. 49° 55' N; long. 0° 57' 42" E.

AIMA'K, one of the principal tribes of Iliyats in Persia. They are said to be of Afghan origin, and are Sunnites in religion. They are chiefly located in the southern parts of Khorásán; and are reputed to number 50,000 houses.—Morier.

AIMARAEZ, a mountainous district of Peru, in the prov. of Lima, extending about 120 m. from N to S, and 26 from E to W; bounded on the W and NW by Andahuilas; on the E by Cotabamba; on the S by Parinacocha; on the SE by Chumbivilcas. It is intersected by the three head-streams of the Pachachaka. It was once an important mining district.
AIMARGUES, or AYMARGUE, a town of France, in the dep. of Gard, and arrond. of Nîmes, 12 m. SW of Nîmes. Pop. 1,950.

AIME, or AISME, a town of Savoy, in the Tarentaise, on the r. bank of the Isère, 9 m. NE of Mortiers, at an elevation of 2,420 ft. above sea-level. Its ancient name was Axima; and some antiquaries identify it with the Forum Claudii of the Romans. | Pop. 1,000.
AIMEN, a lake of Finland, in the prov. of Kymmenegard, to the N of Lake Saima.
AIME'O. See TAHITI.
AIMOUTIERS. See EYMOUTIERS.

AIN, a river of France, which, rising in the Jura chain, near Nozeroy. intersects the dep. to which it gives name in a SSW course, and falls into the Rhone near Anthon, 20 m. above Lyons, after a course of about 90 m. Its principal tributaries are the Bienne, the Oignon, and the Albarine, on the 1.; and the Valouse and Suran, on the r. It is navigable from the bridge of Navoy to its embouchure; and a large quantity of timber and charcoal is annually conveyed by it to the city of Lyons.

AIN, a dep. in the E of France, part of the ancient province of Bourgogne, and comprising the districts of Bresse and Bugey, the former including the principality of Dombes, the latter Gex and Valromey. The geographical position of its capital, Bourg-which, however, is a little to the NW of the centre of the dep.-is N lat. 46° 12', E long. of Paris 2° 53', or of Greenwich 5° 14' nearly. It is separated by the Rhone from Savoy on the E, and from the dep. of Isère on the S; by the Saone from the dep. of the Rhone, and of Saône-et-Loire, on the W; on the NW it has the dep. of Saone-et-Loire; and on the N that of Jura. Bisected from N to S by the river from which it takes its name, this dep. is naturally divided into two districts of nearly equal extent. Its eastern division, lying between the Ain and the Rhone, is intersected by numerous branches from the Jura range, some of which rise to the height of from 4,000 to 5,000 ft. above sea-level, presenting various degrees of cultivation, and enclosing plains and valleys of great fertility. In this division rises Mont Credo, the extremity of the Jura range. The western division, the ancient Bresse and the Dombes, encircled by the Ain and Saone, is generally level, but furrowed into numerous ridges, many of which form the channels of small streams. The soil of these plains, consisting of a thin layer of vegetable mould upon a basis of compact impermeable clay, is marshy and unproductive; but the valleys, in which there is a good calcareous alluvium, are sufficiently fertile to supply the wants of the inhabitants. Near the middle of the dep. the lake of Nantua, with a superficies of 662 acres, has an elevation of 1,300 ft. above sea-level.—The dep. of A. comprises a superficial area of 592,674 hectares, or 1,464,497 English acres, of which more than twofifths are under cultivation. The proportion of permanent meadow-land to arable is nearly a third; and the vine-district comprises not less than a fifteenth of all. Woods and forests occupy about two-ninths of the entire surface of the dep. The annual produce of grain amounts to about 1,950,000 hectolitres of 29.837 Winchester bushels. This dep. exports three-fifths of its wines, of which the most esteemed are those of Belley, and the white wines of Pont-de-Veyle. The culture of filiaceous plants has been successfully pursued of late years, and that of the mulberry yields a rich return in the produce of the silk-worms. The rearing and fattening of cattle afford extensive employment. The pigs and fowls of Bresse form important articles of export. At Naz, near Gex, is an establishment for rearing sheep of a fine fleece. The domestic animals are numerous and their breeds generally good. The oxen reared in the rich pastures of Reyssouse and Chalaronne are highly prized by graziers. The wild boar is sometimes, but rarely met with, and game is not very abundant. The rivers abound with fish, and are particularly celebrated for their shad and trout. In the arrond. of Belley are paper and spinfactories; in Nantua there are similar manufactories, and in Bourg are some of porcelain and cloth. Leather is manufactured in all parts of the country. There are 453 fairs annually held in this dep. Cattle, fowls, grain, shoes, cloth, &c., are the principal articles of sale or exchange at these. The Rhone, though navigable from Lyons to Parc near Seyssel, affords in its slow and winding course but small facilities for navigation; but the Saone is highly valuable to the industry of the dep., being navigable throughout. The Ain is only navigable when swollen by the rains. The oak, beech, and fir of the forests afford important articles of commerce. An iron mine at Villebois-sous-Belley is the only one in operation in the dep.; but quarries of marble, freestone, gypsum, chalk, and clay are numerous, and the best lithographic stones in France are procured in the arrond. of Belley. Turf is dug in some localities; and bitumen at Seyssel and Pyrimont.—The dep. of A. is divided into the five arrondissements of Bourg, Belley, Gex, Nantua, and Trevoux, which are the names also of its principal towns. Its most celebrated locality is Voltaire's Ferney. It is also subdivided into 35 cantons, and 442 communes. A. belongs to the seventh military division, of which the general rendezvous is at Lyons; and its superior tribunals are held in the royal court of that city. It is a diocese, of which the episcopal seat is at Belley, and which is a suffragan of the archbishopric of Besançon. It returns 5 deputies, and is divided into 5 electoral arrondissements, of which the principal towns are Pont-de-Vaux, Bourg, Trevoux, Belley, and Nantua. The number of electors in 1846 was 1,203. The pop. of the dep. in 1821 was 388,828. In 1841 it was 355,694, being at the rate of 60.02 per square kilomètre; while the mean average pop. of all France in 1841 was 64.868 per sq. kil. It contributed to the revenue of 1844, 7,650,772 francs. The vulgar dialect is the Auvergnat. Its topographical position has always rendered this dep. a peculiar locality, marked by the maintenance of many singular local customs. Successively invaded, ravaged, or occupied by foreigners or by neighbours, and always a frontier-province, it has preserved in different districts, the manners, characters, and customs of its different occupants.—Lateyssonniere, Recherches historique sur le dep. de l'Ain, 4 vols. in 8vo, 1838-43.- Dict. Geog. Univ.—Patria.

AIN, the Hebrew, Arabic, Syriac, and Ethiopic term for fountain or spring, and hence of frequent occurrence in Eastern topography, in combination with other names: in all which cases it intimates the present or former existence of some fountain or spring of water near the place. The following is by no means a complete list of geographical names compounded of this word and some special adjunct; but it enumerates the more important localities in which the term occurs:

AIN (RAS EL), a small v. in the neighbourhood of three fountains,-sometimes known as 'the Cisterns of Solomon,'-which extend in a line of about a furlong from E to W, 3 m. S of Tyre, the modern Sur, in Syria. They are filled with clear water which springs from a flat, parched, sandy soil. The mouth of the principal well is an octagon of 61 ft. diameter; and the water rises up to the brink of the well-which is elevated 15 ft. above the plain-with such force and abundance, that, after issuing from an outlet which has been broken on the W side, it forms a rivulet which turns several mills in its passage to the sea. Buckingham says that Maundrell's description of these fountains "applies as accurately to their present state, as to that of the day on which it was written."

AIN (RAS EL), or AIN VERDEH, a v. of Asiatic Turkey in the district of Diar Rabiaa, on the Khabur river, 82 m. S by E of Diarbekir. The Khabur has its source partly in a multitude of little springs near this town, and partly at a greater distance to the NW.
AIN-EL-AALAK, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Damascus, 9 m. NE of Kara.
AIN AANAB, a v. of Asiatic Turkey, in the pashalik of Acre, 58 m. NE of Acre.
AIN ABU MABAD, a v. on the S coast of Arabia, on a bay about 6 m. deep, between Ras el Kosair and Ras el Asidah; consisting of a mosque, and about 100 huts. Springs abound here.—Haines.

AIN ABU SUWEIRAH, a v. near the E coast of the gulf ning mills, and some silk, linen, and straw-hat manu- of Suez, at the mouth of Wadi Wardan; in that part of the peninsula of Sinai which has been identified with the wilderness of Etham, in N lat. 29 deg. 32 min. Burckhardt says the lower parts of Wadi Wardan, extending 6 or 8 m. in breadth, consist of deep sand. Upon small sandy mounds in this plain tamarisk-trees grow in great numbers, and in the midst of these rises the well of Szoueyra, about 2 m. from the sea.
AIN AGAN, a v. of Tripoli, in the district of Barca, on the E coast of the gulf of Sulphur; in N lat. 30 deg. 38 min. There are some wells of brackish water here. - Beechey.
